headline: CATTLE CRATER WHILE GRAINS MARK TIME
subheadline: Feeder cattle drop 1.8% as livestock complex unravels, grains stay range-bound ahead of Thursday's planting data.
lead: Cattle fell through $241 to close at $240.85 while corn held flat at $4.79 for a fifth straight session, and the divergence is getting loud. Feeder cattle dropped 1.8% to $356, dragging live cattle down 1.1% in a coordinated livestock selloff that has no weather excuse, no disease catalyst, just technical damage feeding on itself. The grain complex refuses to follow, wheat up six cents, beans gaining a penny, acting like the planting calendar still matters more than fund flows.
the_takeaway: Cattle breakdown accelerating while grains ignore the livestock mess entirely.
teaser: Cattle crash continues while corn holds the line in a split-personality session.

one_number.context: Japan's refineries hit 73% utilization for the first time since March as strategic oil releases and non-Middle East crude supplies ease the supply crunch. The recovery signals global energy markets are adapting to Strait of Hormuz tensions, which helps explain why crude held above $101 despite ongoing geopolitical uncertainty.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called hogs' six percent explosion a classic short squeeze with fundamental support underneath.
yesterdays_call.note: Hogs gave it all back today, falling 1.5% as the squeeze reversed into technical selling.

sections.title: Cattle Breakdown Accelerates
sections.body: Live cattle broke $241 and never looked back, closing $240.85 down 1.1% in the heaviest volume of the month. **Feeder cattle led the damage at $356, down 1.8%**, as the entire livestock complex unraveled without a fundamental catalyst to blame. The breakdown has technical momentum now, managed money liquidating long positions built when cattle was trading $252 two weeks ago. Box beef cutouts haven't moved, fed cattle trade hasn't reset, but the chart's in charge and the funds are getting out.
sections.catalyst: Trump delays beef tariff suspension amid political criticism
sections.bottom_line: Technical damage feeding on itself, no fundamental excuse needed anymore.

sections.title: Grains Ignore The Mess
sections.body: Corn closed flat at $4.79, the fifth session inside a 15-cent range, acting like the livestock crater isn't happening three screens over. **Wheat gained six cents to $6.78, beans up a penny to $12.28**, both showing the grain complex has its own calendar and it's called Thursday's planting progress report. December corn-July spread held 23 cents of carry, pricing prevent-plant risk that cattle traders aren't thinking about. The funds that fled livestock aren't rotating into grain, they're going to cash.
sections.catalyst: House votes today on nationwide E15 expansion legislation
sections.bottom_line: Range-bound patience while livestock burns, planting calendar still runs the show.

sections.title: Energy Holds Geopolitical Premium
sections.body: Crude gained 80 cents to $101.85 as a Chinese tanker tested safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz, proving the chokepoint is navigable but still pricing risk premium. **Natural gas added half a cent to $2.83**, barely moving as Europe's storage mandate fight with Brussels stays theoretical. Japan's refinery utilization hitting 73% shows the market is adapting to non-Middle East supply chains, but the adaptation costs money and crude's holding above $100 until the Hormuz situation resolves completely.
sections.catalyst: Chinese tanker tests Strait of Hormuz passage amid ongoing tensions
sections.bottom_line: Premium stays until Hormuz tensions actually resolve, not just adapt.

outside_the_pit.title: Senate Agriculture plans Farm Bill 2.0 text in June
outside_the_pit.body: Chairman Boozman says he'll release the Senate's version of the skinny farm bill next month with markup to follow. The timeline puts final passage into late summer, which means 2026 crop marketing decisions get made under current law assumptions.

outside_the_pit.title: Food inflation jumps 0.5% in April CPI report
outside_the_pit.body: Food-at-home prices rose 0.7% as grocery inflation returns after March's flat reading. The acceleration gives the Fed another reason to keep rates elevated, which keeps the dollar strong and export sales under pressure.

outside_the_pit.title: BP takes 40% stake in Uzbekistan oil blocks
outside_the_pit.body: The energy giant's move into Central Asian production is part of the broader supply chain diversification away from Middle East dependence. More non-OPEC production coming online means less leverage for traditional oil exporters.

spread_to_watch.label: Live cattle / feeder ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: The ratio dropped below 0.68 for the first time since March as feeders fell harder than live cattle. When feeders lead lower, it signals problems with the replacement cattle market, which means the entire cattle cycle is repricing downward.

basis.headline: Corn basis steady, livestock basis widening on cash pressure
basis.body: Eastern Belt corn basis holding firm as ethanol plants return from spring maintenance, keeping local premiums intact despite flat futures. **Cattle basis widening to discounts** as packers pull bids lower, cash trade struggling to find footing after the futures breakdown. Hog basis following cattle wider as the entire meat complex reprices.

weekly_thread.question: Will corn planting hit 45% by Friday to stay on the five-year average?
weekly_thread.status_text: Tomorrow's USDA report becomes the week's pivot point. Early state reports suggest the Belt is tracking close to normal pace, but Thursday's national number decides whether the 22-cent carry stays or collapses.

the_more_you_know.title: When livestock leads grain volatility (and when it doesn't)
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 1.8% feeder crash had zero impact on corn, which stayed flat at $4.79 despite cattle and hogs selling off hard. The disconnect shows grain's got its own fundamental drivers right now, planting pace and weather, that matter more than cross-commodity fund flows. But when grain harvest starts in September, livestock feed demand becomes the tail that wags the corn dog. The seasonal determines which market leads.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress report; corn above 40% planted keeps the calendar on track
Weekly export sales; corn under 500K MT signals demand concern
Fed cattle cash trade; under $240 breaks another technical floor

daily_quote.text: Success is to be measured not so much by the position that one has reached in life as by the obstacles which he has overcome.

source_summary: USDA futures settlements, livestock cash markets, energy complex, CPI data, commodity positioning reports, ag policy developments
